hero.headline: Every CN code has a reason. Aimport shows you the reasoning.
hero.sub: We automate the import compliance workflow — classification, documentation, and client communication — with a defensible reason behind every result.
hiw.head: From product data to a code you can defend.
step1.h: The code — and the reason behind it.
step1.line: Describe a product. Aimport walks the GRI rules to the right CN/TARIC code, shows the reasoning, and saves it to your audit trail.
step1.sub: Add units and unit cost to see landed cost at the live duty rate.
step2.h: Catch the risk before the auditor does.
step2.line: Upload a client's invoice. Aimport verifies each code and flags descriptions that are incomplete or hard to defend.
step3.h: Let the client fill the gaps.
step3.line: Missing a detail? Send a one-tap link. The client answers structured questions — and the completed file returns to you.
step4.h: A defensible file, ready to hand over.
step4.line: Aimport packages the code, its GRI reasoning, and the legal basis into export-ready files — formatted for your declaration software and ready to send to a customs officer.
step4.sub: One record, defensible end to end: from a product description to a filing anyone can follow.
mkr.legal_item: Section XV Note 1 · CN Explanatory Note to 7308 90
exp.sub: Steel mounting bracket · GRI 1 → 6 · legal basis attached
exp.ret: ↑ upload to your system · send to customs
quote.body: No more chasing five separate emails. One click shares the file with the importer — and the missing descriptions come back filled in.
quote.cap: The one-tap survey from Resolve: Aimport asks the client the right questions, and you get a complete, defensible file back.
sav.s1: 3–4 hours reduced to under 15 minutes · industry benchmark
sav.s2: manual average 20 min · AImport average 2 min
sav.l3: duty value in penalties for misclassification
sav.close: A broker handling 50 declarations a week recovers roughly 75 hours of classification time per month.
sav.foot: Based on Polish customs broker salary data (61 PLN/hr) and industry-reported time reductions.
who.c1_p1: You carry the classification reasoning in your head. Aimport lays it out in the order you would defend it.
who.c1_p2: Read the path, check the rule at each branch, sign off or override.
who.c2_p1: You need the same answer every time, no matter who runs the classification.
who.c2_p2: Aimport applies one rule set to every product and keeps the trail, so an audit reads your decisions instead of guessing at them.
who.c3_p1: Classification follows from what your product is: its material, its function, its degree of processing.
who.c3_p2: Aimport shows which facts drive the heading, so you know the classification before the goods ship.
who.c4_p1: A declaration without reasoning is hard to audit.
who.c4_p2: Aimport produces a classification trail in GRI order, the same structure you use to assess whether a code holds up.
who.close: When you want to see how it handles your product categories, you ask for access.
faq.head: What brokers ask first.
faq.q1: Does the model ever invent a code that isn't in the tariff?
faq.a1: No. At each branch the engine hands the model the candidate headings it pulled from the CN and TARIC tables, and the model picks one or asks for a fact. A code outside that list cannot reach the result, because the model never writes the digits itself.
faq.q2: What happens when my description leaves out a deciding fact?
faq.a2: The engine stops at the branch where the facts run out and asks one question, the same question a classifier would ask: knitted or woven, steel or aluminium. You answer, and the reasoning continues from that branch instead of starting over.
faq.q3: Can I override the engine at a branch?
faq.a3: Yes. Each branch shows the candidates the engine weighed and the rule it applied. Pick a different candidate and the trail records your choice, then the reasoning continues from there. You stay the declarant, and the engine shows its work either way.
faq.q4: Does it give me the duties and trade measures, or only the ten digits?
faq.a4: Both. The engine returns the code, then the measures that attach to it for the origin you enter: the third-country duty, any anti-dumping or countervailing duty, suspensions, and quotas. You see the rate you would declare on entry, not the heading on its own.
faq.q5: Does Aimport check existing binding tariff rulings?
faq.a5: Yes. It searches the EBTI database for binding rulings on goods like yours and shows them next to the result. You see how customs classified comparable products before you commit to a heading, and you spot a conflict before an officer does.
faq.q6: How does it handle parts, sets, and composite goods?
faq.a6: The engine works through GRI 2 to 4 in order: unfinished and disassembled articles under GRI 2(a), composite goods and sets by their essential character under GRI 3(b), and the tie-break under GRI 3(c). The trail names the rule that settled the case, so you can defend the call.
faq.q7: Who carries responsibility for the final classification?
faq.a7: You do. The declarant signs the declaration and owns the result. Aimport gives you the reasoning to check and the trail to defend, and it files nothing on your behalf.
access.head: Bring your own product categories.
access.p: Aimport is in pilot with a small group of customs brokers and freight forwarders. If you work in import compliance and want to see how it handles your product categories, write to us.

cls.guide: Describe what you're importing. The more detail — material, function, use — the more precise the classification.

cls.ph_desc: Describe the product as specifically as you can — material, function, intended use.
